events.label: reasoning
events.text: We are asked to assess a drywall & finishing condition: "joints not staggered". The work is rough-complete. Reference: GA-216, IRC. We need to determine if it's within tolerance. Joints not staggered is a defect because drywall joints should be staggered to avoid continuous seams that can crack. According to GA-216 (Gypsum Association standard) and IRC (International Residential Code, though this is commercial, but IRC often referenced for residential, but GA-216 is for commercial too), joints should be staggered. Specifically, GA-216-2018 says: "Joints shall be staggered. End joints of adjacent boards shall be offset not less than 12 inches (305 mm) in field of wall and ceiling." So if joints are not staggered, it's a defect. The condition is "installed-defective". The work stage is rough-complete (roughed in). So decision: fail. Risk: medium? If joints are not staggered, it could lead to cracking, so risk is medium or high. I'd say medium because it's not an immediate safety hazard but a durability issue. Component conditions: installed-defective. Percent complete: maybe 80%? Rough-complete means installed but not finished. Value: no numeric reading, so 0. Confidence: high, 0.9. Event time, rate, order, workable: not needed. Findings: "Drywall joints are not staggered, creating continuous seams that are prone to cracking." Actions: "Remove and reinstall drywall with staggered joints per GA-216." Rationale: "GA-216 requires end joints to be offset at least 12 inches; non-staggered joints are a defect." References: ["GA-216 Section 5.3", "IRC R702.3.1"] (IRC might have similar). I'll output JSON.
